subject: Physics
q:
Two short bar magnets of length 1cm each have magnetic moments 1.20 A m2 and 1.00 A m2 respectively. They are placed on a horizontal table parallel to each other with their N poles pointing towards the south. They have a common magnetic equator and are separated by a distance of 20.0 cm. The value of the resultant horizontal magnetic induction at the mid-point O of the line joining their centres is close to
( Horizontal component of earth's magnetic induction is 3.6×10-5  Wb m-2 )
question:
Two short bar magnets of length 1cm each have magnetic moments 1.20 A m2 and 1.00 A m2 respectively. They are placed on a horizontal table parallel to each other with their N poles pointing towards the south. They have a common magnetic equator and are separated by a distance of 20.0 cm. The value of the resultant horizontal magnetic induction at the mid-point O of the line joining their centres is close to
( Horizontal component of earth's magnetic induction is 3.6×10-5  Wb m-2 )
solution:
B net = B 1 + B 2 + B H

= μ 0 4 π M 1 x 3 + μ 0 4 π M 2 x 3 + B H

= { 10 −7 ( 1.2+1 ) } ( 0.1 ) 3 +3.6× 10 −5

= 2.56 × 10 - 4  Wb / m 2

question: The magnetic susceptibility of a material of a rod is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>499</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Permeability in vacuum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>Hm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Absolute permeability of the material of the rod is
solution:
μ=μ01+xm

=4π×10-7×500

=2π×10-4 Hm-1

subject: Physics
q: A paramagnetic sample shows a net magnetisation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>m</mi></math> when it is placed in an external magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></math> at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math>. When the sample is placed in an external magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></math> at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math>, then the magnetisation will be :
question: A paramagnetic sample shows a net magnetisation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>m</mi></math> when it is placed in an external magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></math> at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math>. When the sample is placed in an external magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></math> at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math>, then the magnetisation will be :
solution:
According to Curie's Law,
χ∝1T
Therefore, χ1T1=χ2T2
Also, χ=MB
So, 
M1T1B1=M2T2B2
On substituting the values,
6×40.4=M2×240.3

Therefore, M2=0.75 A m-1

subject: Physics
q: The susceptibility of a paramagnetic material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn></math>. The permeability of the material in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, is [Permeability of free space <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>]
question: The susceptibility of a paramagnetic material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn></math>. The permeability of the material in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, is [Permeability of free space <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>]
solution:
Using the relation of magnetic susceptibility χ and relative permeabilityμr.

μr=1+χ=1+99=100

Magnetic permeability, μ=μ0μr

=4π×10-7×100

μ=4π×10-5

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as
Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R
Assertion A: A bar magnet dropped through a metallic cylindrical pipe takes more time to come down compared to a non-magnetic bar with same geometry and mass.
Reason R: For the magnetic bar, Eddy currents are produced in the metallic pipe which oppose the motion of the magnetic bar.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as
Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R
Assertion A: A bar magnet dropped through a metallic cylindrical pipe takes more time to come down compared to a non-magnetic bar with same geometry and mass.
Reason R: For the magnetic bar, Eddy currents are produced in the metallic pipe which oppose the motion of the magnetic bar.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is false but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is true

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and R are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is false

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>
solution:
Due to change in flux, eddy current is produced which opposes the motion in the metallic pipe for the bar magnet.

Eddy currents are loops of electrical current induced within conductors by a changing magnetic field in the conductor according to Faraday's law of induction. Eddy currents flow in closed loops within conductors, in planes perpendicular to the magnetic field.

Hence, R is the correct explanation and both statements are true.

subject: Physics
q: A metallic rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in North-South direction and is moved at a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> towards East. The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field at that place is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The emf induced in the rod is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mV</mi></math>.
question: A metallic rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in North-South direction and is moved at a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> towards East. The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field at that place is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The emf induced in the rod is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mV</mi></math>.
solution:
Emf produced is ε=BVL⊥v⊥

We know BVBH=tan45°, so BV=4×10-3 T

Then,  ε=4×10-3×20×10-2×20 =16 mV

subject: Physics
q: The percentage increase in magnetic field (B) when space within a current carrying solenoid is filled with magnesium (magnetic susceptibility $\left.\chi_{\mathrm{mg}}=1.2 \times 10^{-5}\right)$ is :
question: The percentage increase in magnetic field (B) when space within a current carrying solenoid is filled with magnesium (magnetic susceptibility $\left.\chi_{\mathrm{mg}}=1.2 \times 10^{-5}\right)$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \% \text { change in } B=\frac{B_{\text {new }}-B_{\text {old }}}{B_{\text {old }}} \times 100 \% \\ & =\frac{\mu \mathrm{ni}-\mu_0 \mathrm{ni}}{\mu_0 \mathrm{ni}} \times 100 \%=\frac{\left(\mu-\mu_0\right)}{\mu_0} \times 100 \% \\ & =\frac{\left(\mu_0 \mu_{\mathrm{r}}-\mu_0\right)}{\mu_0} \times 100 \% \\ & =\left(\mu_{\mathrm{r}}-1\right) \times 100 \% \\ & =\chi_{\mathrm{n}} \times 100 \% \\ & =1.2 \times 10^{-3} \%\end{aligned}$

question: An example of a perfect diamagnet is a superconductor. q:
Statement I : The ferromagnetic property depends on temperature. At high temperature, ferromagnet becomes paramagnet.

Statement II : At high temperature, the domain wall area of a ferromagnetic substance increases. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Statement I : The ferromagnetic property depends on temperature. At high temperature, ferromagnet becomes paramagnet.

Statement II : At high temperature, the domain wall area of a ferromagnetic substance increases. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
solution: As temperature increases, domains disintegrate so ferromagnetism decreases and above curie temperature it become paramagnet.

subject: Physics
q: The length of a magnet is large compared to its width and breadth. The time period of its width and breadth. The time period of its oscillation in a vibration magnetometer is $2 \mathrm{~s}$. The magnet is cut along its length into three equal parts and three parts are then placed on each other with their like poles together. The time period of this combination will be
question: The length of a magnet is large compared to its width and breadth. The time period of its width and breadth. The time period of its oscillation in a vibration magnetometer is $2 \mathrm{~s}$. The magnet is cut along its length into three equal parts and three parts are then placed on each other with their like poles together. The time period of this combination will be
solution:
Time period of vibration, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{M B}}$
Where $\ell=$ moment of inertia of magnet, $\mathrm{M}=$ magnetic moment $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{m} \ell^2}{12}$ and $\mathrm{M}=$ pole strength $\times \ell$
$$
I^{\prime}=\frac{1}{12}\left(\frac{m}{3}\right)\left(\frac{\ell}{3}\right)^2 \times 3=\frac{\mathrm{l}}{9}
$$
and $M^{\prime}=$ pole strength (will remain the same) $\times(\ell / 3) \times 3=M$.
$$
\mathrm{T}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\sqrt{9}}=\frac{2}{9} \mathrm{~s} .
$$

subject: Physics
q: The coercivity of a magnet is $5 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. The amount of current required to be passed in a solenoid of length $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the number of turns 150 , so that the magnet gets demagnetised when inside the solenoid is _____A.
question: The coercivity of a magnet is $5 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. The amount of current required to be passed in a solenoid of length $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the number of turns 150 , so that the magnet gets demagnetised when inside the solenoid is _____A.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{ni}}{\mu_{\mathrm{o}}} \\ & 5 \times 10^3=\frac{150}{30} \times 100 \times \mathrm{i} \\ & \frac{50}{5}=\mathrm{i} \\ & \mathrm{I}=10\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A short bar magnet is placed in the magnetic meridian of the earth with North Pole pointing north. Neutral points are found at a distance of 30 cm from the magnet on the East-West line, drawn through the middle point of the magnet. The magnetic moment of the magnet in Am2 is close to:

(Given μ04π=10-7 in SI units and BH= Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field =3.6×10-5 Tesla.)
question:
A short bar magnet is placed in the magnetic meridian of the earth with North Pole pointing north. Neutral points are found at a distance of 30 cm from the magnet on the East-West line, drawn through the middle point of the magnet. The magnetic moment of the magnet in Am2 is close to:

(Given μ04π=10-7 in SI units and BH= Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field =3.6×10-5 Tesla.)
solution:
Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is along geographical south its North.

M→= Magnetic dipole moment.



At neutral point on the equatorial line the magnetic field B′ cancels BH

∴B′=BH

⇒μ04πMx3=BH 

⇒10-7×M301003=3.6×10-5

⇒10-7×103×M27=3.6×10-5

M=27×3.6×10-510-4

M=27×3.6×10-1

=9.72

M≈9.7

subject: Physics
q: Choose the correct option.
question: Choose the correct option.
options: True dip is not mathematically related to apparent dip.
True dip is less than apparent dip.
True dip is always greater than the apparent dip.
True dip is always equal to apparent dip.
solution:
If the apparent dip circle is at an angle α with true dip circle then,

tanϕ'>tanϕ ⇒ϕ'>ϕ

Therefore, apparent dip angle is greater than true dip angle.

Hence, true dip (ϕ) is less than apparent dip ϕ'

subject: Physics
q: A bar magnet of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in the magnetic meridian with its north pole pointing towards the geographic north pole. A neutral point is obtained at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the center of the magnet. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>H</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi></math>, the magnetic moment of the magnet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A bar magnet of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in the magnetic meridian with its north pole pointing towards the geographic north pole. A neutral point is obtained at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the center of the magnet. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>H</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi></math>, the magnetic moment of the magnet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
i.e. 2μ04πmr2×7r=0.4×10-4

⇒2×10-7×m×772+1823/2×104=0.4×10-4

m=4×10-2×3733/214

⇒M=m×14 cm=m×14100=0.04×3733/214×14100

⇒M=4×10-4×7203.82=2.88 J T-1.

The current required to be passed in a solenoid of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and number of turns <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math>, so that the magnet gets demagnetised when inside the solenoid is
question: The coercivity of a small magnet, where the ferromagnet gets demagnetised is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. solution:
For solenoid, the magnetic field needed to be magnetised the magnet.

B=μ0nI

where, n=100,l=10 cm=10100 m=0.1 m

⇒3×103=1000.1×I

⇒I=3 A

subject: Physics
q: A solenoid having area $A$ and length ' $l$ ' is filled with a material having relative permeability 2. The magnetic energy stored in the solenoid is :
question: A solenoid having area $A$ and length ' $l$ ' is filled with a material having relative permeability 2. The magnetic energy stored in the solenoid is :
solution: $\frac{\mathrm{U}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^2}{2 \mu_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{u}_0} \Rightarrow \mathrm{U}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^2}{4 \mu_0} \mathrm{~V}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^2}{4 \mu_0} \mathrm{~A} \ell$

question:

subject: Physics
q: A paramagnetic material has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>28</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>atoms</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its magnetic susceptibility at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>350</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is <math><mn>2.8</mn><mo>Ã—</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its susceptibility at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is
question: A paramagnetic material has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>28</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>atoms</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its magnetic susceptibility at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>350</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is <math><mn>2.8</mn><mo>Ã—</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its susceptibility at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is
solution:
From Curie's law, the magnetic susceptibility is inversely proportional to the temperature, i.e.,

χ∝1T

Hence, the ratio of magnetic susceptibility at different temperature T1=350 K and T2=300 K.

χ2χ1=T1T2

χ2=χ1×T1T2=2.8×10-4×350300

χ2=3.267×10-4.

subject: Physics
q: An aeroplane, with its wings spread <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is flying at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in a horizontal direction. The total intensity of earth's field at that part is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> The emf induced between the tips of the plane wings will be

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Consider a ferromagnetic material :
Assertion (A) : The individual atoms in a ferromagnetic material possess a magnetic dipole moment and interact with one another in such a way that they spontaneously align themselves forming domains.
Reason (R): At high enough temperature, the domain structure of ferromagnetic material disintegrates. Thus, magnetization will disappear at high enough temperature known as Curie temperature.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Consider a ferromagnetic material :
Assertion (A) : The individual atoms in a ferromagnetic material possess a magnetic dipole moment and interact with one another in such a way that they spontaneously align themselves forming domains.
Reason (R): At high enough temperature, the domain structure of ferromagnetic material disintegrates. Thus, magnetization will disappear at high enough temperature known as Curie temperature.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Assertion (A) states that individual atoms in a ferromagnetic material possess a magnetic dipole moment and interact to form domains. This is a correct description of the domain theory of ferromagnetism, where exchange interactions lead to spontaneous alignment of moments within small regions called domains.
Reason (R) states that at high temperatures (above the Curie temperature $T_C$), the domain structure disintegrates due to thermal agitation, and the material becomes paramagnetic. This is also a correct statement regarding the temperature dependence of ferromagnetic materials.
However, Reason (R) describes the effect of temperature on the domain structure, whereas Assertion (A) describes the fundamental nature and formation of domains in the ferromagnetic state. The disintegration of domains at high temperature is not the reason why they form spontaneously at lower temperatures. Therefore, both statements are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : The diamagnetic property depends on temperature.

Statement II : The induced magnetic dipole moment in a diamagnetic sample is always opposite to the magnetising field.

In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : The diamagnetic property depends on temperature.

Statement II : The induced magnetic dipole moment in a diamagnetic sample is always opposite to the magnetising field.

In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are False
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is true
Statement I is correct but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution:
The properties of a diamagnetic material are as follows:

1. The susceptibility has a low negative value.

2. Susceptibility is independent of temperature.

3. The relative permeability is slightly less than one.

4. When placed in a non-uniform magnetic field they have a tendency to move away from the field.

5. When suspended freely in a uniform magnetic field, they set themselves perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field.

Hence, Statement I is false, but statement II is true.

subject: Physics
q: Certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of non magnetic metallic material. The function of this metallic material is
question: Certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of non magnetic metallic material. The function of this metallic material is
options: to oscillate the coil in magnetic field for longer period of time
to bring the coil to rest quickly
to produce large deflecting torque on the coil
to make the magnetic field radial
solution: Certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of nonmagnetic metallic material. When the coil oscillates, the eddy currents generated in the core oppose the motion and bring the coil to rest quickly. Eddy currents are loops of electrical current induced within conductors by a changing magnetic field in the conductor according to Faraday’s law of induction. Eddy currents flow in closed loops within conductors, in planes perpendicular to the magnetic field.

subject: Physics
q:
A solenoid has a core made of material with relative permeability $400$. The magnetic field produced in the interior of solenoid is $1.0$ T. The magnetic intensity in SI units is $\alpha \times 10^5$. The value of $\alpha$ is ______.
(Free space permeability $\mu_0=4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI units.)
question:
A solenoid has a core made of material with relative permeability $400$. The magnetic field produced in the interior of solenoid is $1.0$ T. The magnetic intensity in SI units is $\alpha \times 10^5$. The value of $\alpha$ is ______.
(Free space permeability $\mu_0=4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI units.)
solution:
The magnetic field $B$ inside the solenoid is related to the magnetic intensity $H$ by the formula:

$B = \mu H = \mu_0 \mu_r H$

Rearranging for $H$, we get:

$H = \dfrac{B}{\mu_0 \mu_r}$

Substituting the given values ($B = 1.0$ T, $\mu_r = 400$, and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ T m/A):

$H = \dfrac{1.0}{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 400}$

$H = \dfrac{1.0}{1600\pi \times 10^{-7}}$

$H = \dfrac{1}{16\pi \times 10^{-5}}$

$H = \dfrac{1}{16\pi} \times 10^5$ A/m

Comparing this with the given expression $H = \alpha \times 10^5$, we find:

$\alpha = \dfrac{1}{16\pi}$

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{16\pi}$
